planting.note: Plant container vines near a very sturdy support structure in spring; trumpet vine is vigorous and heavy — it will pull down lightweight trellises. Control root suckers by mowing or digging promptly, as the vine can spread aggressively.

directions: Plant near a very sturdy fence, masonry wall, pergola, or large tree in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil — trumpet vine is extraordinarily adaptable and drought tolerant; the large orange-red trumpet flowers appear from June through September and are among the most important native nectar sources for ruby-throated hummingbirds in the eastern US. The vine climbs by aerial roots and is very vigorous — growing 20–30 feet and sending up root suckers widely around the base. Control by mowing suckers, cutting back hard annually, and avoiding planting near the foundation of structures where the roots can damage masonry. The sap causes skin irritation in many people. Despite its aggressive character, trumpet vine is exceptional in rural settings where it can be allowed to cover a fence, outbuilding, or dead tree. The contact dermatitis risk from sap is real; wear gloves when pruning.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; purchase only nursery-propagated plants. The starchy trunk contains cycasin toxins and was traditionally processed through extensive washing before use as food starch.

directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; coontie is the only cycad native to continental North America and the only host plant for the spectacular Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a striking Caribbean species with iridescent blue-green wings that was once thought extinct in Florida before recovering after landscape plantings of coontie increased. This prehistoric-looking plant with stiff, dark green pinnate fronds emerges almost stemless from a large underground trunk and is extraordinarily drought, heat, and salt-spray tolerant once established in zone 8–11. The large red seeds are produced in dramatic cones and are toxic; plant away from areas accessible to children and pets. Plant coontie specifically to support Atala butterfly populations in South Florida.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in lean, very well-drained soil in full sun; in zone 9–11 it naturalizes as a self-sowing perennial subshrub.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun after temperatures warm; blue porterweed is a star butterfly plant of the tropical Gulf Coast, producing long slender spikes with intense blue-purple flowers that open progressively up the spike from the base throughout summer and fall. The flowers are critically important for gulf fritillary, zebra longwing, and monarch butterflies; hummingbirds also visit the spikes regularly. In zone 9–10 it naturalizes as a weedy self-sowing subshrub in disturbed, sandy areas — appropriate for a wild, naturalistic butterfly garden but not a formal border. The common name 'porterweed' comes from the dark, beer-like color of the steeped leaves historically used as a beverage. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage additional blooming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; excellent drainage is essential — this plant rots in heavy, moist clay. Excellent in gravel gardens and rock gardens.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; hummingbird mint is a native sage relative of the Texas-New Mexico border region with the most extraordinary foliage fragrance in the genus — a pure, sweet bubblegum-spearmint scent that fills the air when brushed. The deep rose-pink tubular flowers from summer through frost are consumed almost exclusively by hummingbirds, which are so attracted to the flowers that the plant earns its common name. It is genuinely drought tolerant and thrives in hot, dry conditions where it has excellent drainage. The plant self-sows modestly and is short-lived as a perennial (3–5 years); take cuttings in summer to maintain the garden. Deer resistant. An excellent choice for zone 8–9 pollinator gardens in full sun with excellent drainage.

planting.note: Surface sow seed 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; seeds need light to germinate. Can also sow in fall for natural stratification. Plants are dioecious — both male and female plants produce flowers, but only females set seed.

directions: Surface sow seed in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; purple meadow rue is one of the tallest and most architecturally striking native shade perennials, producing airy sprays of lavender-purple to white flowers on dark purple stems in late spring to early summer that rise above bold, blue-green, aquilegia-like foliage. It grows naturally in moist prairies, stream banks, and open woodland edges across the Midwest and Great Plains, tolerating light shade well. The flowers are pollinated by wind and native bees. The plant provides excellent vertical structure in the middle-to-back of a woodland edge border and combines beautifully with native ferns and woodland wildflowers. It spreads slowly by rhizome and self-seeding to naturalize in favorable conditions.
